comments.title: Multiple Timelines
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

What are your thoughts on alternate/multiple timelines? Do they exist and if so how do they interact with our pirmary/conscious timeline? How as students of esoterica should we incorporate them into our morality and our metaphysics? Or are they just a wish projection and another convenient way for adherents to bypass the tragedies and vicissitudes of our conscious lived reality?

Thank you!

Anthony from Pittsburgh PA

replies.title: Re: Multiple Timelines
replies.content: I've never encountered any reason to believe that they exist. Certainly the occult traditions I've studied don't include the concept. The furthest they go is to concede that different people experience the world differently and so, in a certain sense, we each live in a slightly (or more than slightly) different world. Yet reality in its fullness embraces all of those experienced worlds and more.

replies.title: Re: Multiple Timelines
replies.content:
Dear Mr Greer

There is this thing in some current new new age/alternative circles called “reality shifting”. The one thing I’ve noticed is that they’re not very good at describing exactly what they mean by this and often talk about moving to a different time line.. Sometimes it sounds perfectly sensible. You can cary out various techniques to change your attitude and the way you think and for example go down a different career path. If you do this you are in a sense going down a different time line from the one you you were on and can can make some changes in the universe.

However, in other cases they seem to claim that you can literally change the reality that you are living in and make it sound as if you can end up in a different universe and you can have whatever you want. If this was true then then I would be living in a 1930’s style Green Utopia where everyone travelled round on steam trains and had perfect 1930’s manners and clothes and lived in perfect 1930’s English villages and the only crimes would be committed by members of the upper or middle classes and would-be solved by the likes of Hercule Poirot or Lord Peter Wimsey. Unfortunately this has never happened, which is why I am still ablate use the internet to communicate with you. I suppose this is what happens when you confuse reality with metaphor.

Jasmine

replies.title: Re: Multiple Timelines
replies.content: That's correct. You can certainly change what I suppose could be called your lifeline, the trajectory you make through the complexities of the world, and some of those shifts can take you into places so different from your current experience that you might as well have entered a different world; most people experience this, for example, when they take up serious magical training. But the rest of the world doesn't necessarily change, and no, you can't always get what you want. (Though if you try sometime, you just might find you get what you need.)

replies.title: Re: Multiple Timelines
replies.content:
Thank you, Mick Jagger!

- Cicada Grove

replies.title: Re: Multiple Timelines
replies.content: Alternate timelines and realities seem like problematic mathematics to explain geocentrism, to me. If people are microcosms and unique, you already have everything alternatives and alternities could provide. My take. Celadon

comments.title: About a Tree
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

Up until May 3rd there was a large, beautiful, healthy Aspen in front of my house (it was 60-80 feet tall). On May 2nd my landlord informed me he was going to cut it down because he thought "it might damage the house". The following morning two men with a chainsaw arrived.

I had called my lawyer on the night of the second to see if I could stop this but it turned out that my landlord was legally within his right to kill this perfectly healthy tree that wasn't hurting anyone (and posed no imminent threat to the house). I could have gotten my lawyer to send a volly of nasty letters but that could have easily cost me thousands of dollars and it was likely that my landlord would prevail in the end.

I went out and talked to the tree the night before it was killed and explained that it was absolutely not my idea to kill it but that there wasn't much I could do. I apologized to the tree and told it that if it wanted to seek retribution to please direct that at my landlord and not my family.

I'm looking for a new place to live because I really don't want to have any more dealings with my tree-killing landlord.

My question is: Should I do anything else for the spirit of the tree or is the talk I had with it the night before it was killed sufficient?

Thanks,

FSH

replies.title: Re: About a Tree
replies.content: What you did was both sufficient and gracious, and the local devas (nature spirits) will have taken note of it. I wouldn't want to be that landlord, and you're probably wise to get as much distance from him as you can.

replies.title: Re: About a Tree
replies.content: Not the OP. How do I handle this if I am gardening and need to remove (to kill) a few saplings, for example, or do weeding?

replies.title: Re: About a Tree
replies.content: Trees are at a different level of consciousness from other plants -- they are to plants what we, porpoises, and a few other species are to animals, the transitional stage to the next rung of the ladder. If you have to take out a sapling, if possible let it know the day before, so that it can withdraw from the material form. Other plants, just be kind and make it quick and clean.

replies.title: Re: About a Tree
replies.content: Unfortunately I did remove a few saplings without doing this. Is there a way, specific to trees, that I might make amends?

replies.title: Re: About a Tree
replies.content: Go to the nearest tree of the same kind, explain what happened, and apologize. That should be wholly acceptable.

replies.title: Re: About a Tree
replies.content: Thank you. I went out earlier and did this, along with offering a blessing, with the three different species in question and it seemed to go OK. I didn't get a negative feeling from any of them. On one of them, right after I apologized, I suddenly noticed an ant and a caterpillar of a type I've never seen before on the bark of its trunk. Thanks again for the suggestion.

replies.title: Re: About a Tree
replies.content:
Piggybacking here. I enjoy gardening but living in a somewhat lush environment, weeding is a big part of the job. It keeps all the plants I grow healthy and happy but I find weeding to be energizing and afterwards at night when dozing off I frequently see the plants I pulled clairvoyantly so I suspect I'm picking up stuff etherically as I work.

Perhaps it's silly but I worry I'm doing something wrong as they seem to energize me at a loss to their own life. Is there something I should do to redirect the energy as I work or is it a case of just thanking them?

replies.title: Re: About a Tree
replies.content: Just thank them. This is the normal end stage of their life cycle and both the souls of the plants and the devas of the garden area are comfortable with it -- the devas will be glad to see it happening since it's being done to allow other plants to thrive.

replies.title: Re: About a Tree
replies.content:
Dear JMG,
Thank you for your kind words and advice.

FSH

replies.title: Re: About a Tree
replies.content:

If it’s any consolation, with some care the tree may survive even this.

replies.title: Re: About a Tree
replies.content:
Thanks for this but my landlord has taken this into consideration and intends to come by today and drill holes into the stump and pour chemicals into them to ensure that the tree dies. I hope the tree prevails regardless.

I honestly can't comprehend putting this much effort into killing a tree that was quite healthy and provided both shade and privacy for the house. It's really just bizarre.

FSH

replies.title: Re: About a Tree
replies.content:
I don't understand it either, but it seems a very common attitude among real-estate-investing types.

The two houses across from ours were purchased by a guy who "does rentals" and watching the renovation process... his whole philosophy of yards seems to be: tear everything out and sod it. The previous owner of the house directly across had rosebushes, large fruit trees, and some hardy landscape plants such as yucca and crape myrtle. Today, thanks to a crazy amount of work with chainsaws and skid steers, there's not so much as a blade of grass to be found there. Every passing car stirs up clouds of dust. It hurts to look at it. I think the idea is to keep everything "low maintenance". But I can't imagine being the sort of person who could rip out producing fruit trees to make it easier to mow. That is completely alien. The whole view out my window now is like a giant open wound, and I close the curtains to avoid glimpsing it.

comments.title: Extremes of Magic
comments.content:
Good Evening, Mr Greer,

I am aware that you are careful not to categorize magic into so-called White and Black Magic; or to judge any magician as to their “morality”.

But I have a question which includes goetia or magic work with demons. All though this may not necessarily apply, specifically I am wondering about A. Crowley and Thelema. My feeling is that the average Thelema follower considers him/herself a practioner of High Magic; i.e. magic with the goal of self-transformation.
Although I realize not all magicians presume to be on the path of High Magic.
How do such magicians who may dabble in the relative negative “morality”, alongside their High Magic, reconcile that negativity with their self-transformation?

Please, magicians, feel free to respond, too.

Thanks

replies.title: Re: Extremes of Magic
replies.content:
I don't use the labels "white magic" and "black magic," and I don't make a habit of judging people, but ethics are as important in magic as sterile technique is in surgery -- neglect either one and things go septic. As for Crowley, well, I'm not a fan. He grew up in the church that invented modern fundamentalist Christianity, and spent the rest of his life rebelling against it, becoming an egomaniac and a world-class jerk in the process. For all the passionate energy he devoted to magic, he could never get over himself, and sooner or later made life miserable for everyone who had to deal with him.

After his death, his legacy was picked up by several more competent mages, Grady McMurtry arguably first among them, who turned the steaming mess into a viable system of initiation and succeeded in marketing it. I know some first-class mages who are Thelemites; I have no idea why they chose that particular path, but hey, do what keepeth thou from wilting shall be the loophole in the law, and all that.

As for goetia, it's best strictly avoided. It's like going in search of your local gang members because you think you can get them to do you favors -- they might grin and comply for the time being, but sooner or later they're going to beat the crap out of you and rob you blind, because that's what gangsters do to the clueless and arrogant. Demons are the same way. Turning to spirits of any kind to get favors is the classic mistake of the failed mage -- everything you can get from them, you can accomplish all by yourself, if you're willing to put in the work and accept the changes that follow. That's the problem, of course, and people have been falling for that trap -- "bow to me and I shall serve thee" -- since ancient times.

replies.title: Re: Extremes of Magic
replies.content: Plenty of times in media, I've seen the trope of a magic-user who attempts to bend demons to his will, only to get dragged down into Hell. The Summoner in Diablo II is one example of many, arguably an indirect analogy is Scar/hyenas from Lion King. Leaving aside that flashy fictional magic is quite unlike the real thing, it's interesting that arcane lore shows up in all sorts of unlikely places.

replies.title: Re: Extremes of Magic
replies.content: That trope is present all through the world's legends and folklore because it happens, if not quite as dramatically as in the stories. Demons -- malevolent disembodied beings -- are a reality, and dealing with them is a great way to mess yourself over. Christopher Marlowe's great Elizabethan play Doctor Faustus is a classic expression of the theme, if you're interested in something pre-modern!

comments.title: Did I do this?
comments.content:
A few weeks ago I complained about an annoying landlady and asked your advice about how to deal with her. You suggested I try invoking Saturn and practice some Saturnine planetary charity. This I did. I invoked Saturn at the appropriate time and gave three dollars to a beggar. I also invoked Saturn on each of the two subsequent Saturdays, wishing to give myself fairly robust and lasting protection.

A few days ago when I went to pay the rent she told me she had just had the worst three weeks of her life. She had suffered from a respiratory affliction, her sister unexpectedly died and she had a nasty fall that bruised her face and left her with a concussion.

The timing seems suggestive. Could this be a case of a person with a history of being too aggressive, something of a bully, and when someone does a bit of magical self-protection it produces for the first party an unexpected karmic wallop? Or is something else going on?

I don’t want to give people concussions, and she’s not altogether a bad sort; but I can’t deny I’ve enjoyed the relative peace.

replies.title: Re: Did I do this?
replies.content: Yes, it's quite possible that she got to deal with some of her own karma, but since that wasn't your intention you don't bear any responsibility for it. The planetary intelligence you invoked assessed the situation and decided that it was time for her to deal with some of her crap; you'll know it worked if she becomes a nicer person. Dion Fortune used to say that Mars was the great initiator of the swollen-headed, but in my experience Saturn is even more effective at taking arrogant people down a peg or two.

comments.title: Failure of LHC
comments.content:
This might be a bit of an overblown title, but it seems to me that the large hadron collider has failed in its mission. It found exactly what it was set up to find, the Higgs Boson, and nothing more. The highly reliable but not particularly beautiful Standard Model of quantum physics didn't generate super symmetry, as the mathematical physicists thought, and no new particles, or even anomalies, have been found.

It's interesting as it means that it may be the limit for high energy particle physics, and especially interesting given that Pluto is fading out. High energy particle physics, the idea that we make nature behave in a large atom smasher seems to me to be the epitome of pluton.

So where does it leave physics? I suspect physics might have to focus again on the big, rather than the small, and that quantum physics, may be seen in times to come as a statistical exercise.

Thoughts would be appreciated.

Hello JMG,

I recently read one of Rudolf Steiner's lectures where he referred to the etheric body as having organs. It struck me as odd. The etheric body always seemed more like a uniform and amorphous aura, without that kind of differentiation.

Are there etheric equivalents of a brain, heart, lungs, skin, etc.?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It's a common misconception to think of the nonphysical planes as being vaguer and more abstract than the material plane. CS Lewis had a useful bit on that in That Hideous Strength, where Jane, one of the protagonists, is coming to terms with the reality of the spiritual:

"For still she thought that 'Religion' was a kind of exhalation or a cloud of incense, something steaming up from specially gifted souls towards a receptive Heaven. Then, quite sharply, it occurred to her that the Director never talked about Religion: nor did the Dimbles nor Camilla. They talked about God. They had no picture in their minds of some mist steaming upward, rather of strong, skilful hands thrust down to make, and mend, perhaps even to destroy."

The higher planes are more complex, more intricate, more structured, and vastly more powerful than the plane of matter. It's the material plane that's vague, formless, indefinite and muddled by contrast. The etheric body does in fact have organs, intricately patterned and active organs, which are sometimes called chakras or energy centers; it also has channels of circulation like the bloodstream, which are sometimes called nadis or meridians. The astral body also has organs, though they're difficult for us to understand because astral substance is in such constant motion; and the mental sheath, the highest of our embodiments so far, is evolving organs.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) The common denominators are will and imagination; everything else is detail. Yes, different systems have different effects, "paranormal" or otherwise; the measurements that allow you to know that you're making progress vary from system to system, but one that's common to all valid systems is that your life improves.

2) A serious practitioner is one who puts in practice time every day. It's the same rule you use to call someone a serious musician: how often do they practice? As for the differences in the experience of life, that would practically take a book to explain. Imagine for a moment that what you think of as "paranormal" abilities were actually normal, things that everyone has though most people don't notice or use. Imagine that what you think of as "supernatural" was all perfectly natural, and you experienced those things all the time. Imagine that you stopped experiencing yourself as a helpless victim of circumstances beyond your control, realized that your will and imagination were shaping your life constantly in ways that you weren't conscious off, and that you started taking conscious control of the process and making your life what you want it to be. Imagine all of that together; that's a start.

replies.title: Re: Ovate grade spagyrics
replies.content:
Not JMG, but...
Absolutely you can take a plant residue and calcine it without having to douse it in alcohol or anything else first. And yes, it WILL smoke and stink a lot but only until the grosser plant fiber material burns off. When you get it to a light, puffy gray ash it won't be smoking anymore and it won't have much if any smell.
Finally, as I mentioned in another MM post, another technique you can use to get a fairly clean vegetable salt is to calcine (burn) your plant as far as you can, that fluffy gay-white ash state, and give it one more grind with a mortar and pestle. Then put it into a jar of distilled water. Make sure it's distilled water. You don't want other mineral salts getting involved here. Put this in a warmish place for a few days. Shake it well at least once a day. After a few days, pour this off through filter paper into, well, I used a Corningwear dish (a casserole, in fact. Nice broad flat bottom. And again, NOT metal. Metal will also contaminate the salt as well as "earthing" the energies you are working with and imparting to the salt by your manpulations), and put this in a warm oven and evaporate off the distilled water. You'll find the vegetable salts crystallized onto the bottom of the dish.If you are feeling especially industrious, you can save the residue from the filter and give it some more fire and/or leech it again in distilled water. Or not. Up to you.

replies.title: Re: Witches
replies.content:
Ahem. The meanings of words are defined by how people use them. Words don't have a fixed meaning enshrined in the heavens; they're sounds used by human beings to communicate things, and their meanings inevitably drift over time. When somebody says that a meme is dank, for example, he doesn't mean that it's wet and mildewed! In the same way, a word like "witch" has no fixed meaning -- it's been used to mean various things down through the years.

Thus it doesn't matter at all how I define the word "witch." There are people who use the word to mean "follower of a neopagan religion," and people who use the word to mean "devil worshipper," and people who use the word to mean "useful piece of construction equipment."

Take your pick.

comments.title: The meaning of invoking the elements in the sphare of protection
comments.content:
A little theory question. I think the attitude of being light in theory and experiencing things for oneself makes a lot of sense in an esoteric path, so before my question I just want to mention I am asking this because of a religious concern, I want to make sure I understand what is it that I do when I invoke an element.

So I tried doing some reading and meditated on what I found, and based on this it seems an "element" is a symbol, meant to train the mind and not inform it. I read that in a Kabbalistic context it refers to a part of the self, analogous to the chemical elements that compose the material body, but not exactly. But the elements make up most other things as well, so my first question, is which object's "elements" am I calling? The "Air" within my own body? within the volume of the sphere of protection? or is this the idea (in the platonist sense) of air, I am talking to the air of everything in existence?

My second question is about the word "invoke". My native language is Hebrew and I know you have a working knowledge of the language. I could not find a suitable translation, but if I had it, it would prove helpful for me so I can meditate on this word. So far it seems it's like calling someone and inviting him in, much like what happens during prayer. If so, and if the "elements" are just symbols, than who am I calling when invoking each element?

I'd like to end by thanking you for teaching this, although so far I have only been doing the opening and closing, it proved a rewarding experience.

replies.title: Re: The meaning of invoking the elements in the sphare of protection
replies.content:
1) An element is a general category of experience, like a color. Lots of different things are yellow; similarly, lots of different things are airy, or watery, or fiery, or earthy. The element, like the color, is simply a way to talk about what these things have in common. as for what you call in the Sphere of Protection, that depends on what you ask for -- it can be as specific or as general as you wish, depending on your choice of the wording.

2) I'm sorry to say you've got an exaggerated sense of my knowledge of Hebrew; I can read the alphabet (or, rather, the aleph-beth) but I have only a very limited vocabulary. To invoke is to call toward you, just as to banish is to send away from you; the Latin prefix in- is more general than the English preposition. As for who you're calling, here again, that depends on you; if you want to call upon the six permutations of YHV from the Sepher Yetzirah, for example, as some Jews I know do when they practice this ritual, why, I don't think there's any question who you're calling!

replies.title: Re: The meaning of invoking the elements in the sphare of protection
replies.content:
Is there a difference between invoke, evoke and conjure or do they all mean to call something toward you?

replies.title: Re: The meaning of invoking the elements in the sphare of protection
replies.content: "Invoke" comes from Latin in-voco, I call towards; "evoke" is from ex-voco, I call out of; "conjure" is from com-juro, I pledge with. You invoke to call something toward you, you evoke to call something up out of something, you conjure by right of pledges made between you and a spiritual being.  In practice they're all basically synonyms, but there are subtle shades of difference.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
That doesn't defy rational explanation at all -- it's just that our rationalists have their eyes clenched shut, their fingers stuffed in their ears, and are going "La, la, la, I can't hear you!" at the top of their lungs to keep from noticing the existence of the normal, natural, perfectly rational human capacity for telepathy. It's especially common between mothers and children, for good evolutionary reasons -- a mother who can hear her child's cry of distress when the child happens to be out of earshot has a major Darwinian advantage!

As for the mechanism, it's important to discard the materialist superstition that holds that minds only exist within lumps of meat called human brains. What we call "thought" or "mental activity" is a continuum that fills space, the way that gravity does; it's concentrated in brains but extends outward from there. So a thought can pass from mind to mind through that mental continuum. In fact, if we learn to quiet our minds and pay attention, we'll realize that we're constantly receiving whispers of other people's thoughts and feelings that way.

...And I hesitate to pop off, because I'm not JMG, and I'm not an advanced member of AODA or any related group, but I just felt I should say this, since it might be helpful for someone to hear.
I tool my first formal steps in esoteric training 45 years ago, and I must be some special block of rock, because I have rolled along with nothing much happening for me for years and years. I was passed along the chairs in my Masonic lodge until I reached the East without, it seemed to me, any particular skill or talent, although some very good things happened for my Lodge during my year, although I attribute that more to my team than any special talent on my part. I was similarly passed up the ladder to the East in a couple of esoteric Orders and have had people put in my care to guide, although I am in fear and trembling every day that my advice or help may hurt them I've seen how an idle word spoken in haste can send a person running off to do crazy s**t that has hurt them badly, and I dread doing that to anyone.
It is only recently that anything has started to happen for me where I feel as though I am gaining insights, or having strange synchronisities, or symbolic dreams, like other people I knew back in my old AMORC Lodge days used to speak about. Otherwise, it has been day after day of meditate, read, study, do exercises, repeat.
I was thinking this over today, and this is the conclusion I've come to: There isn't anything more important I have done with my time than this. So bear in mind, especially if this is early days for you on the Path, that it's like digging a well in a place where the water table is pretty far underground. You just have to pick a spot and start to dig. You will find that you will go a long way with nothing but dirt and gravel coming up. Pebbles, field stones, a pain in the back to haul up and away. Just keep on going. You'll eventually hit MUD! Won't that be fun!?! Heavy, mucky and dirty, but really a sure sign your almost there. It WILL happen one day. I apologize if this long ramble is out of place.

comments.title: Awareness of Influences
comments.content:
As someone who does not (yet) practice magic, is there a way to identify influences and their source/type?

These (i think) are described here as emotional responses to person/place/thing or thoughts that seem to come out of nowhere. Thanks to this forum and starting on some of the reading with Levi I am aware of what I can best call non-material world but obviously it doesn't come with name tags. I had one personally powerful experience that I don't know how to put in words but included synchronicity with music that seems to communicate from a non-human source. I expect there are a lot of more subtle influences that are ongoing.


Possibly related, my personal experience seems to be much different emotionally and mentally than the people I am close enough to talk about these things with. For example I don't really hear voices or think in words but other people describe whole conversations they have with themselves. comments.title: Mundane astrology for sports teams
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

Now that round 2 of the NHL Stanley Cup Playoffs are underway, it got me thinking about astrology as applied to sports teams. Assuming it is possible at all, how would one go about delineating a horoscope for a particular team on a particular game day? Would one treat the team like a nation as you have done in your recent Mars-Saturn conjunction analysis for the United States? Or would one treat the team like a person and compare the natal chart of the team at its founding to the horoscope of the game day to make one's analysis? Might I also be correct that if it is possible and it is drawn like a nation, the position of the sun might relate to the teams captain, the moon to the teams fans, etc? I understand also, that the team is composed of individual men, all of whom receive their own celestial influences, complicating the situation considerably.

A second question as it relates to the occult nature of sports teams, is that some teams appear to have a certain character or behavioral patterns that have been repeated over the course of their history. Certain teams seem to always lose in Game 7 of a best of four series for example, and seem to lose in similar fashions. Would this inherent "character" of a particular franchise be determined (at least in some part) by the natal chart drawn at the date of its founding?

I'm speculating wildly here of course, and I know you do not focus on natal astrology if that is the branch that would cover sports teams, but my curiosity has got the better of me.

All the best,

Egyptian Vulture

replies.title: Re: Mundane astrology for sports teams
replies.content: There are people who do sports astrology, just as there are people who do horse racing astrology and stock market astrology. Since you can make quite a bit of money at all three, they don't give their methods away for free! Since I don't aspire to learn about any one of these, I've never coughed up the very considerable sums I would need to pay to learn the methods.

replies.title: Re: Mundane astrology for sports teams
replies.content: While it's not my particular subject of interest, there exists a relatively easily attainable book "Sports Astrology" by John Frawley, whose "Horary Textbook" I found excellent and useful, and which should probably also be acquired if you really want to follow along the chart examples in "Sports Astrology" properly.

replies.title: Re: Mundane astrology for sports teams
replies.content: Frawley's a Vedic astrologer, isn't he? That may be why I didn't think of him.

replies.title: Re: Mundane astrology for sports teams
replies.content: No, actually he's Renaissance all the way! He may or may not be your cup of tea as he places no truck with Alan Leo and other like astrologers who began adding outer planets into the mix-- he does find a place for the outer planets but only as extra sources of information as opposed to primary foundations in a chart. And he has a tendency to look down on "psychological" interpretations of a chart. To which I say, fair enough, actually, as I applaud efforts to cut away the stuff that are attempts to find new angles that can just tell customers (or the astrologer) what they already want to hear. Anyway if you're not familiar with him you might give him a look. His "The Real Astrology" or "Horary Textbook" are the ones to look at.

comments.title: House cusps
comments.content:
Hi John and others (John, as always, thank you for this space).

Astrology question: how does one interpret an opposition that falls exactly on (non-angular) house cusps? Obviously the method of calculating houses (Placidus vs. Koch etc.) will change the exactness, but that's kind of the point: the technical houses in which the opposition will fall depend on the system used.

Note that this question was prompted by a natal chart, but I figure that insights from mundane astrology would still be illuminating.

I poked around the internet a bit, I found a source that says that if a planet falls within a few degrees of a cusp, then it should be considered as falling in the next house. But my intuition (yes I meditated on it) suggests that the cusp position is not accidental and should have a special interpretation.

The chart that prompted this question really has only two striking features: two major oppositions, each involving a stellium, each falling on a cusp (4:5 vs 10:11 and 5:6 vs. 11:12). It really seems like the karmic signature involves all 6 houses, possibly functioning as a circuit where frustration just gets passed from house to house.

Thanks for your insights!

replies.title: Re: House cusps
replies.content: If you're going to follow your intuition, by all means do so, but the lore known to me is the same thing you found online. If it's exactly on the cusp it belongs to the house of which that is the cusp.

replies.title: Re: House cusps
replies.content:
Good idea to meditate on your intuition.

"the technical houses in which the opposition will fall depend on the system used"
My two sense is remember that if you look up into the sky there are no neat and straight lines. The ideas of different house systems is just that, an idea. I think the 12 sign zodiac is a profoundly powerful but its still a human concept from are love of the number 12. This causes some astrologers to begin shouting that this or that house system is the one true way but I always take it with a grain of salt.

A friend
